segments.s507-g17: بپرسید is a 3rd-person past/perfect form 'he asked' (پرسید) used here as the reporting verb. ازیشان (از + ایشان) is the dative/ablative 'from them' and که introduces the subordinate clause; ایدر is the adverb 'here'. شگفت is a noun 'wonder' (not the adjective) describing what is being asked about. Note the order: reporting verb + indirect object + complementizer + locative + head noun of the embedded question.
segments.s507-g18: چه چیزست is the content of the question: چه ('what') + چیزست (a fused/archaic 'چیز است', 'is a thing' → 'what thing is it'). کاندازه (کان + اندازه) = 'to that degree / to such an amount'. باید is the modal 'ought / must', and گرفت is the past infinitive/simple past 'take / seize' (here used as the verb of obligation). Together they form the indirect question 'what thing is there that to such an extent one must take.'
segments.s507-g19: چنین is an adverb 'thus / so'. داد is the 3rd-person past 'gave' (verb). پاسخ is the direct object 'answer', and بدو ('to him') is the dative. رهنمای is the subject 'the guide / adviser' placed after the verb — a common poetic inversion (subject after verb) meaning 'the guide gave him such an answer.'
segments.s507-g20: که‌ای here functions as the archaic/vocative particle (literally 'که ای') addressing someone: 'O ...'. شاه is 'king', پیروز is an epithet 'victorious', and پاکیزه‌رای is a compound adjective 'پاکیزه' + 'رای' meaning 'pure-minded' or 'of pure opinion'. This segment is the opening of direct speech addressing the king.
segments.s507-g21: شگفتیست is a fused predicate 'شگفتی است' meaning 'it is a marvel / it is wondrous' (presentative/predicative). ایدر = 'here'. که introduces the relative/explanatory clause, اندر = 'in' and جهان = 'the world'. The clause frames the scope: 'a marvel here that in the world...'
segments.s507-g22: کسی here with the negative verb ندید means 'no one' (classical Persian often uses کسی + negative to mean 'no one'). آن = 'it'/'that'. ندید is the past negative 'did not see' (ن + دید). آشکار و نهان are coordinated adverb/adjective phrases 'openly and secretly / manifest and hidden', describing the two modes in which no one has seen it.
segments.s507-g23: درختیست is the fused form 'درختی است' meaning 'it is a tree'. ایدر = 'here'. دو بن گشته literally 'has become two-rooted/trunked' — گشته is the perfect/passive participle 'has become / has turned into', and بن 'root / base' with دو 'two' describes the twin bases. جفت follows as an appositive 'a pair / paired', describing the tree as dual.
segments.s507-g24: که introduces the reason/explanation clause: چونان 'such / like this'. شگفتی here is 'a marvel' (noun). نشاید is an impersonal/modal form 'it is not fitting/possible' (negative of شایَد), and نهفت is the participle/adjective 'hidden' (so نشاید نهفت = 'cannot be hidden' or 'it should not/must not be concealed').
segments.s507-g1: ز راه بیابان is a prepositional phrase 'from the desert road' (ز + noun phrase). به شهری رسید is a past‑tense clause with the verb رسید (3rd sg. past) meaning 'reached/arrived' and the prepositional phrase به شهری 'to a city' (شهری used as 'city' here). The whole segment is V final after the locative prepositional phrase.
segments.s507-g2: ببد شاد is a past copular/result clause 'he became/was glad' (ببد = became/was, شاد = happy). کآواز is the relative/causal connector ک- attached to آواز ('the voice/sound'), and شنید is the verb of the subordinate clause 'he heard' placed at the end (classic Persian verb‑final subordinate). Together it expresses 'he became glad because/when he heard the people's voice.'
segments.s507-g3: همه بوم و بر is a nominal phrase meaning 'the whole land and region' (همه = all, بوم و بر a fixed pair meaning land/territory). باغ آباد بود is the predicate: باغ (garden) + آباد (prosperous/inhabited) with بود (past of 'to be'), yielding 'was prospering with gardens' or 'was full of gardens.'
segments.s507-g4: در مردم is locative 'among/in the people.' از خرمی gives the cause/source 'from/with mirth,' and شاد بود is the predicate 'were happy' (شاد = happy, بود = past copula). The order is locative + causal phrase + predicate: 'Among the people, because of mirth, they were joyful.'
segments.s507-g5: پذیره شدندش is a verbal phrase meaning 'they received him' or 'they accepted him'; پذیره (reception/acceptance) with شدند (past plural auxiliary) plus the enclitic ـش = 'him' marks the object. بزرگان شهر follows as the agent/subject 'the great ones of the city' (verb preceding subject is common in epic style).
segments.s507-g6: کسی را is the accusative/topic 'someone' (را marks definite/affected object). که introduces a relative clause 'who,' and از مردمی بود means 'was of the people' (از = from/of, مردمی = of the people, بود = was). بهر at the end is an old/formal preposition meaning 'for' or 'for the sake of' and likely links this object to the purpose or reason implied next in the narrative.
segments.s507-g7: برو here is an interjection/imperative 'go on' or an encouraging exclamation; همگنان is the subject 'companions/peers.' آفرین خواندند is a past plural clause 'they cried “afarin” (bravo)' with خواندند (3rd pl. past) used as 'uttered/called out.' The overall sense is encouragement and applause from his fellows.
segments.s507-g8: همه زر و گوهر is the object phrase 'all the gold and jewels' (همه = all; زر = gold; گوهر = gem/jewel), and برافشاندند is the past plural verb 'they scattered/showered' (برافشاندند = they scattered/showered down). The verb comes after its objects, as typical in Persian narrative.
segments.s507-g113: This is a descriptive nominal phrase with an implied copula. به سنگ درم = “on/at a stone of dirham” where درم qualifies سنگ (a gem/stone described by its value); هر یکی شست من = “each one (was) the thumb of mine,” i.e. “each was as large as my thumb.” Note that Persian places the possessor after the noun (شست من = “my thumb”) and often omits the verb “to be” in such descriptions.
segments.s507-g114: ز زر و ز گوهر = “of/from gold and of/from jewels,” where the repeated preposition ز marks material or source. یکی کرگدن = “one rhinoceros” (here likely a single ornamental/figurative object made of those materials). There is no copula; the phrase describes an object composed of gold and gems.
segments.s507-g115: بپذرفت is a past narrative verb meaning “he accepted/took” (here contextually “he captured/took”). زان شهر = “that city” (زان = that/that one), and و لشکر براند is a coordinated clause “and (he) drove away the host/army.” Persian often omits the explicit subject; both verbs are third-person past actions.
segments.s507-g116: ز دیده = “from the eye(s),” i.e. “from his eyes.” همی is the classical progressive/continuous particle meaning “continually/keeps” (here giving a vivid, ongoing sense). خون دل = a genitive compound “blood of the heart” (a poetic image for deep-sorrow tears). برفشاند is a past-tense verb ‘scattered/spattered’ (he scattered/bedewed), so the whole means he made his heart’s blood spatter from his eyes.
segments.s507-g49: همی is a durative/adverbial particle marking ongoing or habitual action ('kept on / was continually'). راند is a poetic past 3rd‑person verb 'rode/drove' (here 'rode on' or 'rode along'). با + noun indicates accompaniment ('with'). رومیان is the plural noun 'Romans' and نیک‌بخت here follows it as an adjective/noun 'fortunate' (i.e. 'the fortunate Romans' or the narrator’s epithet for the rider); word order is subject/adjunct then verb, typical in older Persian narrative.
segments.s507-g50: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'. آمد is the simple past 'came' (3rd singular). به + نزدیک expresses motion or approach 'toward / near'. گویا is a particle meaning 'as if / it seems' (introducing a simile). درخت is the noun 'tree' that completes the simile ('seemed like a tree').
segments.s507-g51: زمینش is 'its ground/earth' (زمین + enclitic -ش). ز (here poetic) means 'because of / from'. گرمی is 'heat' or 'warmth'. همی again marks continuous action. بردمید is a poetic past-form verb meaning 'was lifted / carried off / torn away' — here describing the earth's surface reacting to heat.
segments.s507-g52: ز is again 'from / because of'. پوست is 'skin / hide' (here likely animal hides). ددان in the provided lexicon entries does not match the contextual meaning 'beasts/animals' (the lemma list appears mismatched), so its sense is marked unknown; را is the definite object/ topical marker that attaches to the preceding word or phrase. خاک 'soil/earth' is the noun, پیدا 'visible/found' is adjectival/verbial, and ندید is the past negative 'did not see' (here 'was not seen' or 'could not be seen').
segments.s507-g53: ز + noun + پرسید is a light construction meaning 'he asked (from) the speaker' = 'he asked the speaker'. کین here is an archaic/dialectal demonstrative 'this' (not the common noun 'vengeance'); that particular sense is not present in the provided sense list, so it is marked -1. پوست چیست is a straightforward predicative question 'what is this skin?' with ست as the copula 'is'.
segments.s507-g54: ددان را uses را as a definite/object/topic marker; the lemma/senses supplied for ددان do not match the contextual meaning (likely 'the beasts' or 'givers') so its sense is marked -1. برین گونه is a frozen phrase 'بر این گونه' meaning 'in this manner/so' (here the ین is the archaic/orthographic form of 'این' functioning as the demonstrative). درنده is an adjective 'ferocious / predatory'. کیست is the interrogative 'who is (it)?' with ست copula.
segments.s507-g55: چنین 'thus/so' introduces the manner of the reply. داد is the past verb 'gave' used here as 'gave (an) answer' (i.e. 'replied'). پاسخ is the noun 'answer/reply'. بدو is the enclitic combination 'to him' (= به + او). نیک‌بخت here is a noun/epithet 'the fortunate one' (the speaker/answerer). Word order is emphatic: manner + verb + object + indirect object + subject/title.
segments.s507-g56: که introduces a reported clause 'that / namely'. چندین is an indefinitary numeral/adjective 'several / many a'. پرستنده is 'worshipper / devotee' (here plural implied). دارد is the present/past auxiliary 'has' (present sense 'has' here). درخت is the subject 'the tree' (word order here is verb-final for emphasis: 'that the tree has many worshippers').
segments.s507-g25: This is a nominal clause identifying the two trunks: 'یکی' (one) + noun 'ماده' (female) and parallel 'دیگری' (the other) + 'نر' (male). The final 'اوی' is a classical third‑person pronoun referring back to the tree/trunk (′it′) and functions like a resumptive pronoun: 'one (was) female and the other (was) male (of it)'. Word order is somewhat inverted and elliptic — the copula 'بود' is omitted here but supplied in the next segment.
segments.s507-g26: Here the verb 'بود' ('was') appears with the predicate 'سخن‌گو' (literally 'speech-speaker' = speaking/speaking one) followed by the subject 'شاخ' (bough/branch), a common epic inversion (predicate before subject). 'با رنگ و بوی' is a prepositional phrase ('with color and scent') describing the bough. 'سخن‌گو بود شاخ' thus = 'the bough was a speaker.'
segments.s507-g27: 'به شب' is a temporal prepositional phrase meaning 'at night'. The verb phrase 'ماده گویا و بویا شود' uses the classical subjunctive/passive form 'شود' ('becomes') with two predicate adjectives: 'گویا' (here 'able to speak/speaking') and 'بویا' (an archaic adjectival/participial form relating to scent). The clause means 'by night the female becomes speaking and scented.'
segments.s507-g28: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause ('when'). 'روشن شود' = 'it becomes bright' (روشن + شود). The clause continues with 'نر گویا شود' where 'نر' (the male) is the subject and 'گویا شود' repeats the predicate pattern 'becomes speaking'. The verb 'شود' is the same subjunctive/presentive form used for simple present/future sense in epic narration.
segments.s507-g29: The proper name 'سکندر' is the subject. The verb 'بشد' is an epic/past narrative form of 'شد' (here 'he went/appeared/set out' or 'he became'), used to narrate past action. 'با سواران روم' is an accompaniment phrase 'with the horsemen of Rome/Byzantium.'
segments.s507-g30: This is a noun phrase in apposition describing those who accompanied him: 'همان' ('those/the same') + 'نامداران' ('renowned ones'). 'آن مرز و بوم' ('that border and land') is a genitival locator specifying which region they belong to. The sequence is determiner + epithet + locative phrase.
segments.s507-g31: 'بپرسید' is an epic past narrative verb meaning 'he asked' (note the archaic prefix b-). 'زیشان' = 'from/of them' (prepositional/complement). The conjunction 'که' introduces the content clause (indirect question), and 'اکنون درخت' begins that clause ('now the tree ...'), with the noun 'درخت' placed after the temporal adverb as the clause's topic/subject.
segments.s507-g32: 'سخن کی سراید' is the indirect interrogative: 'when will speech be uttered' — 'کی' = 'when' and 'سراید' is the classical present/subjunctive form meaning 'will utter/compose/sing' (here 'utter'). 'به آواز سخت' is an adverbial phrase of manner: 'به' + noun ('آواز' voice) + adjective ('سخت' harsh/severe), meaning 'in a harsh voice.'
segments.s507-g89: بپرسید is a 2nd‑person plural/formal imperative here (’ask’). از introduces the source/target of the asking (’from’/’of’), ترجمان is the noun 'interpreter', and پادشا (an older form of پادشاه) modifies ترجمان to mean 'the king’s interpreter'. Word order is verb → preposition phrase (typical Persian imperative + object/goal).
segments.s507-g90: که‌ای is the fused relative/vocative construction (که + ای) addressing someone: 'O you who...'. مرد روشن‌دل و پارسا is a nominal phrase in apposition describing that addressee; روشن‌دل is a compound adjective 'clear‑hearted/bright‑minded' and پارسا is an attributive adjective 'pious/virtuous'. The coordinating و simply links the two adjectives.
segments.s507-g91: یکی here means 'one' / 'someone' (a single person) and introduces the person to be asked. بازپرسش (the noun) is the person to be questioned: 'an investigator/judge'. که introduces an indirect question ('whether/that'). باشم is the subjunctive/present form of بودن used here for a first‑person deliberative/optative sense ('should I be / I should go'); به + place (روم) marks direction ('to Rum/Byzantium').
segments.s507-g92: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. پیش آید is a verbal phrase where پیش is an adverbial element and آید (from آمدن) means 'comes/happens' — together 'when it comes/happens'. آن گردش روز is the nominal phrase 'that turning/circuit of the day' (گردش = 'turn/cycle', روز = 'day'). شوم is first‑person subjunctive/potential of شدن 'become/I may be', expressing the speaker's hoped‑for or feared result ('I may become / I will be').
segments.s507-g93: مگر here functions as a negative/exceptive conjunction 'unless'. زنده is the predicate adjective 'alive'. بیند (from دیدن) is subjunctive/present 'sees' (here expressing the hoped/possible event). مرا is the object 'me' split orthographically into م + را in the provided tokens; را is the definite/object marker attached to 'me'. مادرم is 'my mother' (noun + possessive). The clause means 'unless my mother sees me alive'.
segments.s507-g94: یکی again = 'someone' (an unspecified person). تا here expresses purpose/result 'so that / in order that' (introducing the intended outcome). به رخ = 'to the face' (به marking direction), برکشد is a present/subjunctive/imperative verbal form meaning 'may he/she lift/draw up' (بر- + کشیدن) and governs the object چادرم 'my veil' (چادر + possessive ـم). The sequence expresses: 'let someone, so that (my mother) may lift my veil to her face.'
segments.s507-g95: چنین 'thus/so' frames the manner of speech. گفت here functions as the verb 'said/spoke' (the provided sense list treats گفت as 'conversation', but in context it is the past verb 'he said'); با شاه means 'to/with the king' (با marks the addressee). گویا in this position is comparative/simile 'as if' or 'seeming to be', and درخت is the simile 'a tree' — the speaker is described figuratively as 'like a tree'.
segments.s507-g96: که introduces reported or direct speech ('that' / 'namely'). کوتاه کن is an imperative: کوتاه (adjective/verb root 'short') + کن (imperative 'do') → 'make short' or 'shorten'. روز is the object/time 'the day'. و is the coordinating 'and'. بربند is an imperative verb meaning 'bind up/pack up/close' (بر- + بستن/بندان). رخت here means 'baggage/garments/belongings' (pack your things). Together: 'Shorten the day and pack up your belongings.'
segments.s507-g105: 'به شهر اندرون' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into the city's inner quarter' (به + location). The direct object 'هدیه‌ها' ('the gifts', plural marked by -ها) follows, and the past-tense verb 'ساختند' (3rd person plural) comes at the end — here 'ساختند' means 'set up/placed/arranged' rather than 'build'.
segments.s507-g106: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones/nobles'. The preposition 'بر' governs 'پادشا' and here functions as 'toward/to' (motion or approach), and the verb 'تاختند' is past plural 'they rushed/hastened' (an energetic motion verb rather than necessarily violent attack in this context).
segments.s507-g107: The phrase begins with 'یکی' ('one' / 'a certain') followed by the noun 'جوشنی' (a coat of mail). 'بود' is the past copula 'was', and 'تابان' is a predicative adjective 'shining'. 'چو نیل' is a simile 'like indigo', with چو introducing the comparison.
segments.s507-g108: The locative phrase 'به بالای و پهنای' ('in/with respect to height and breadth') uses 'به' in a locative/manner sense to relate dimensions; 'بالای' = 'height/top' and 'پهنای' = 'width/breadth'. 'چرم پیل' is a compound 'elephant hide' (چرم = hide, پیل = elephant). The whole phrase qualifies the preceding armour's dimensions.
segments.s507-g109: دو (the numeral) precedes the noun دندان and quantitatively modifies it: 'two teeth/tusks.' دندان پیل is a noun‑noun juxtaposition (poetic omission of the ezafe) meaning 'the elephant's tusks.' برش پنج shows inverted word order for metrical/poetic effect — normally پنج برش — so 'five sections/cuts.' The final بود is the past copula establishing existence ('there were/it had').
segments.s507-g110: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('which' / 'so that'), referring back to the item just described. آن را is the demonstrative 'it' plus the definite/object marker را (object marked before the verb). به + infinitive (به برداشتن) produces a gerundial sense 'in/for lifting,' and رنج بود is a predicative construction meaning 'was a hardship' — together: 'which it was a hardship to lift.' Word order is typical classical/poetic Persian, with the object phrase (آن را) placed before the verbal phrase.
segments.s507-g111: This is a parallel listing: 'زره بود و دیبای پرمایه بود' — 'there was armor, and there was a substantial/rich brocade.' Persian poetry often repeats the copula بود for cadence. دیبای پرمایه is noun + adjective without an overt ezafe (poetic compression); پرمایه qualifies دیبا as 'substantial' or 'rich.'
segments.s507-g112: ز here is the poetic form of از ('from/of') and + noun زر + کرده (past participle 'made') yields 'made of gold.' آگنده is a predicative/adjectival past participle meaning 'filled' or 'laden' and sits before its noun (poetic order) — آگنده صد خایه بود = 'it was filled with a hundred khāye.' The final بود is the copula. Note the absence of ezafe between آگنده and the following noun, a typical poetic compression.
segments.s507-g65: چنین modifies برگ ('thus/so the leaf'); برگ گویا is a personification ('the speaking/eloquent leaf'), with گویا functioning as an adjective meaning 'speaking/eloquent' here. چه گوید همی is a present-habitual clause: چه = 'what', گوید = classical present 'says', and the particle همی marks continuous or habitual action ('keeps saying').
segments.s507-g66: که introduces the content of what is said ('that'); دل را uses را as the definite/object marker on 'heart'. به + شود (here شوید, archaic subjunctive/present of شدن) expresses change 'become/turn into', with به indicating direction into something. همی again marks the continuity/habitual aspect.
segments.s507-g67: چنین داد پاسخ is a compact past clause 'thus gave an answer / thus replied' (داد = past of دادن). که here introduces the quoted or reported speech that follows; the sequence که‌ای نیک‌بخت functions as a vocative: que+ای = 'that: O...' addressing someone as 'O fortunate one.'
segments.s507-g68: همی گوید is present-habitual 'keeps saying' (همی marking continued action, گوید = 'says'). این برگ شاخ درخت is a noun phrase 'this leaf of the branch of the tree' (word order: demonstrative + noun + possessor sequence placing 'branch' and then 'tree').
segments.s507-g69: که again introduces reported content ('that'); چندین + سکندر is a partitive/indefinite 'many a Alexander' or 'several Alexanders'. چه here is a rhetorical 'how/what' and پوید is a present-form verb meaning 'pursues/appears/comes about' (classical present imperfect), with به دهر 'in/through the world/age' as a locative phrase.
segments.s507-g70: که برداشت is a continuation of the reported clause: برداشت (past/nominal sense 'took' / 'removed') + از نیکویهایش 'from his excellences/virtues' (از = from). بهر is a preposition 'for / to the purpose of' indicating motive or advantage. The clause says roughly 'who takes from his good things for (their own) sake.'
segments.s507-g71: ز شاهیش is a genitive construction 'from/of his kingship' (ز = from/of, شاهیش = 'his kingship' with the possessive suffix). چون سال شد بر دو هفت is a temporal clause 'when it became a year and two sevens' (دو هفت = 'two sevens' i.e. two weeks); شد is the past of شدن used to mark the completed state.
segments.s507-g72: ز تخت بزرگی = 'from the throne of greatness' (ز = from/of, تخت = throne, بزرگی = greatness/grandeur). ببایدش is a fused modal 'it must (be) for him' (بباید + ش enclitic referring to him), and رفت is the past of رفتن 'went/left' — together indicating necessity/obligation that he depart the throne.
segments.s507-g73: Subject first (سکندر). The clause ز + دیده indicates source ('from the eye(s)'); ببارید is the 3rd-person past of باریدن ('to fall; to rain'), used here for drops of blood. In Persian the obligatory possessive is omitted and is understood from context (i.e. 'his eyes'). The noun خون follows the verb for strong, poetic emphasis: 'from his eyes blood fell.'
segments.s507-g74: دلش = دل + enclitic ش ('his heart'). گشت is the past form 'became' (classical equivalent of شد here). پر is an adjective 'full (of)' placed before the noun it qualifies; درد is the content ('pain' / emotional sorrow). از marks cause/reason ('because of / on account of') and رهنمون is the noun 'guide/guidance' giving the source of the pain.
segments.s507-g75: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that / thereafter.' به کس نیز literally 'to anyone also' — combined with the verb نگشاد (negated past of گشودن) it yields 'did not open (his mouth) to anyone,' i.e. he did not speak to anyone. لب is the object placed at the end ('open his lip' = 'speak'). Note the negative prefix نـ on نگشاد marking negation in classical Persian.
segments.s507-g76: پر از غم = 'full of sorrow' (پر 'full' + از marking the content). همی بود is an older/habitual or continuous imperfective construction ('remained/was still'); تا + نیم‌شب sets the temporal limit 'until midnight.' The whole clause says his lips stayed full of grief until that hour.
segments.s507-g77: سخن‌گوی شد is 'became a speaker' (سخن‌گو + شد 'became'). The subject (برگ دیگر درخت = 'another leaf of the tree') follows the verb, a common poetic word order where the predicate appears before the noun it applies to. The noun phrase lacks an explicit ezafe but is understood as 'the other leaf of the tree.'
segments.s507-g78: دگر باره is an adverbial phrase 'once more / again.' پرسید is the simple past 'asked.' زان here functions as 'of/from that' or 'to that' in the sense 'of that (one),' so پرسید زان نیک‌بخت = 'it asked that fortunate one' (i.e. repeated its question to the lucky/fortunate person).
segments.s507-g79: چه introduces an interrogative 'what.' گوید همی is the present/habitual verb 'says' (همی marking continuous/habitual aspect); این دگر شاخ is the subject 'this other branch.' The final گفت here functions as a reportive/nominalizing element ('that was said' / 'said'), so the whole unit reads roughly 'what this other branch says' in reported form. (In classical poetic syntax verbs and reportive markers can co-occur for emphasis or to form reported speech.)
segments.s507-g80: سخن‌گوی 'the speaker' is the subject; بگشاد is the past form 'opened/revealed' (ب‑ prefix + گشادن). راز is the direct object 'secret.' از نهفت literally 'out of concealment' (از = 'from/out of'; نهفت = 'hidden/concealed'), so the clause means 'the speaker unveiled/revealed the hidden secret.'
segments.s507-g41: چنین داد پاسخ is a compact, slightly archaic word order: چنین (thus) + داد (gave, past) + پاسخ (answer) = “gave such an answer.” کزو is the fused classical form of ز + او (from/of him), and بگذری is the 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of گذشتن used conditionally (“if you pass/by”). Together کزو بگذری = “if you pass by him/from him.”
segments.s507-g42: ز رفتنت is a prepositional phrase (ز = “because of/from” + رفتنت “your going/your departure”). کوته شود is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive/passive-like construction (کوته “short” + شود “becomes/will be”) meaning “will be shortened.” داوری (judgment) appears after the verb in stylistic inversion: “by your leaving the judgment will be shortened.”
segments.s507-g43: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” ز + و here represents classical ز + او (from him), rendered as two tokens in the data; برگذشتی is the past 2nd-person of گذشتن with the prefix بر- meaning “you passed by.” نماندت is a negative past form (here impersonal/use with intransitive meaning “did not remain”) and جای (place) follows it by inversion: “when you passed by him, no place remained.”
segments.s507-g44: کران جهان is a genitive noun phrase “the edge/limit of the world.” خواندش is past tense خواند + clitic ش (him) meaning “called him” or “summoned him,” and رهنمای (guide) is the agent/noun; the phrase is inverted for poetic effect: “the guide called him (at) the world’s edge.”
segments.s507-g45: بیابان و تاریکی are coordinated subjects (“wilderness and darkness”). آید is 3rd-person singular present of آمدن “comes,” used here even with two nouns (a common poetic agreement). به پیش is an adverbial phrase “forward / before / ahead of (us).”
segments.s507-g46: به سیری is an adverbial phrase “to/for satiety/satisfaction.” نیامد is past negative “did not come”; the construction places the verb before the (indefinite/negative) subject کس, so the meaning is “no one came (to satisfaction) from his life” — i.e. nobody departed satisfied or escaped with their life content. از جان خویش is a possessive phrase “from his own life” (خویش split into خوی + ش in the data).
segments.s507-g47: نه ... نه is correlative negation (“neither ... nor ...”). کس here with نه means “no one.” دید is past “saw” and applies with the following prepositional phrase از ما (“of/from us”). هرگز modifies شنید (“ever heard”), so the two clauses read “no one of us saw it, nor ever heard (of it).”
segments.s507-g48: که introduces a reported subordinate clause (“that …”). دام و دد و مرغ is a coordinated list (trap, beast/enemy, and bird). بر ره پرید is a compact past action: بر (on/upon) + ره (way) + پرید (leapt/jumped/flew) — here پرید is used generically “leaped onto the road” (applicable to animals and bird), so the whole subordinate clause means “that trap and beasts and bird jumped onto the road.”
segments.s507-g9: همی گفت is a poetic/imperfective narrative formula meaning “kept saying” or “was saying” (همی = continual/iterative + گفت = said). هرکس is the subject “each one” (here ‘each person said’). که‌ای شهریار is a vocative phrase: که (here introducing the quoted speech) plus ای (vocative) before شهریار “O king.” Word order places the report verb before the quoted address.
segments.s507-g10: This is the content of the quoted speech. انوشه is the name/object being referred to. که introduces the clause ‘that/which’ (i.e. ‘what you have done’). کردی is 2nd person past “you did.” بمابر is a contracted poetic form of بر ما ‘upon us/to us’ meaning ‘to our care.’ گذار is an imperative or jussive ‘put/leave (it)’ — together meaning ‘leave it to us.’
segments.s507-g11: بدین شهر is a prepositional phrase ‘to/into this city’ (بدین = به + این in classical/poetic spelling). هرگز نیامد سپاه is a negative past: هرگز ‘never’ + نیامد ‘did not come’ with سپاه ‘an army/host’ as the (logical) subject — literally ‘to this city an army never came.’ Word order places the prepositional phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s507-g12: نه هرگز is another negative emphatic ‘nor ever.’ شنیدست is an archaic perfect/passive form meaning ‘has been heard’ or ‘has heard’ (here functionally ‘has been heard’). کس in this negative context means ‘anyone’ (no one has the experience). نام شاه = ‘the name of a king.’ The whole clause: ‘nor has anyone ever heard the name of a king.’
segments.s507-g13: کنون = ‘now.’ کامدی is an archaic/poetic perfect/present of آمدن (here ‘has come’ or ‘has arrived’). جان ما = ‘our soul/heart/desire.’ پیش تست is an archaic locution meaning ‘is before you’ (تست standing for تو هستی/ت هستی — ‘thou art’), so the phrase states that the heart’s desire is now before the addressee.
segments.s507-g14: که introduces a clause expressing a wish/blessing: روشن‌روان is a compound epithet ‘bright‑souled’ (happy/cheerful in spirit). بادی here is an archaic 2nd‑person subjunctive/coptative of بودن meaning ‘may you be’ (not the literal ‘windy’). و = ‘and’. تن درست = ‘a sound/healthy body’ or ‘be well in body’ (درست = sound/proper). The clause as a whole is a benediction: ‘may you be of bright spirit and healthy body.’
segments.s507-g15: سکندر is the subject ‘Alexander.’ دل از مردمان is a possessive/genitive expression ‘the hearts of the people’ (دل = heart; از + noun marks source/possession here). شاد کرد is a simple past causative ‘made (them) happy’ (شاد = happy; کرد = did/made). The verb applies to the preceding noun phrase: ‘Alexander made the people’s hearts glad.’
segments.s507-g16: ز راه بیابان = ‘from/by the way of the desert’ (ز = poetic from/through; راه بیابان = desert road). تن آزاد کرد = ‘freed the body’ or ‘relieved his person’ (تن = body/self; آزاد کرد = made free). Together this says that by/through the desert route he relieved or freed his body — i.e. removed the burden or reached freedom of body after the wilderness journey.
segments.s507-g81: چنین داد پاسخ که is a short clause meaning “gave this reply: that…”. چنین is an adverb “thus/so”; داد is the past-tense verb “gave” used with پاسخ “reply/answer”; که is the complementizer introducing the reported content that follows.
segments.s507-g82: همی گوید اندر جهان فراخ is the content clause introduced by که. همی is a habitual/adverbial particle “continually/always”; گوید here is the present “says/speaks” (classical present); اندر is the preposition “in/within”; جهان فراخ is the noun phrase “the wide world.”
segments.s507-g83: از آز فراوان نگنجی همی is a clause giving cause or condition. از آز فراوان = “from/excessive greed”; نگنجی is an older negative present/second-person form of گنجیدن meaning “(you) do not fit / are not satisfied / cannot be contained”; همی adds habitual/continuous sense “always/ever.”
segments.s507-g84: روان را چرا بر شکنجی همی is an interrogative-address to the addressee. روان را uses را as the definite/object marker (“the soul” as direct object); چرا = “why”; بر is the preposition “upon/against”; شکنجی here functions as an archaic present/verb-form meaning “(you) torment” (or a deverbal noun with an implied verb) and همی gives the habitual sense — so literally “Why do you put the soul upon torment?”
segments.s507-g85: ترا آز گرد جهان گشتن است is structured as a predicate with a dative/accusative-formed topic. تـ (ت) + را = ترا = “you” as object/topic; آز = “greed”; گرد جهان گشتن is an infinitival/gerundial phrase “to go/roam around the world” (گرد = around, جهان = world, گشتن = to go/wander); است is the copula “is.” Together: “For you, greed is roaming the world.”
segments.s507-g86: کس آزردن و پادشا کشتن است is a nominal infinitival clause in apposition describing that greed: کس آزردن = “to harm people/anyone” (کس = any person, آزردن = to hurt); و = “and”; پادشا کشتن = “to kill kings” (پادشا = king, کشتن = to kill); است is again the copula linking these infinitives to the previous predicate.
segments.s507-g87: نماندت ایدر فراوان درنگ is an admonition not to stay. نماندت is an archaic 2nd-person negative form of ماندن used here as a jussive/imperative sense “do not remain / you will not remain”; ایدر = “here”; فراوان درنگ = “much delay / a long tarrying.” Together: “Do not remain here in long delay” (i.e. don’t linger here long).
segments.s507-g88: مکن روز بر خویشتن تار و تنگ is a second admonition. مکن is the prohibitive “do not”; روز is the object “day(s)/time” (here: one’s days/life); بر خویشتن = “upon yourself”; تار و تنگ is an idiomatic paired descriptor meaning “constricted/tight and narrow” describing روز. Altogether: “Do not make your days upon yourself narrow and tight” → don’t live to make your life cramped.
segments.s507-g97: The pair 'نه ... نه' marks a neither...nor negation; here the first 'نه' attaches to the clause beginning with 'مادرت'. 'مادرت' is 'مادر' + enclitic '-ت' = 'your mother'. 'بیند' is a 3rd-person present/future-form of دیدن used in literary Persian meaning 'will see' / 'sees'. The prepositional phrase 'به روم' attaches to the verb and gives the place/realm ('in/to Rome') where they will not see you.
segments.s507-g98: 'نه' continues the neither...nor negation into this following noun group. 'پوشیده' is a past-participial adjective here meaning 'veiled/covered'; it modifies 'رویان' (people/faces). In Persian poetry the modifier and noun can form a nominal phrase that continues the previous verbal negation (i.e. 'nor the veiled-faced of that place'). 'آن مرز و بوم' is a fixed pair meaning 'that border and soil' = 'that land/realm'.
segments.s507-g99: 'به شهر کسان' is a locative phrase: 'به' with 'شهر' and 'کسان' gives the setting 'in/to the city among the people'. 'مرگت' = 'مرگ' + enclitic '-ت' = 'your death'. 'آید' is a 3rd-person present/future 'comes/will come'. The short clause ends with 'نه دیر' ('not late'), an idiomatic negation meaning 'it will not be long' or 'it won't be delayed' (i.e. it will happen soon).
segments.s507-g100: The verb 'شود' (from شدن) here functions as 'will become' and appears before the nominal list (poetic inversion). 'اختر و تاج و تخت' is a triad of nouns ('star' = fortune, 'crown', 'throne') acting as the subject. 'از تو سیر' is a prepositional phrase ('sated/of you') with 'از' + pronoun 'تو'; 'سیر' is used adjectivally/nominally ('sated, weary') so the whole clause means they will become weary/sated of you.
segments.s507-g101: 'چو' is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. 'بشنید' is the simple past 'he heard'. 'برگشت' is a past tense verb 'he returned/turned back'. 'زان' = 'from that / thereupon' links to the following noun phrase 'دو درخت' ('those two trees'), indicating the place or thing he turned away from.
segments.s507-g102: 'دلش' = 'دل' + enclitic 'ش' = 'his heart'. 'خسته گشته' is a compound of the adjective 'خسته' + 'گشته' (become) meaning 'had grown weary' (perfect/resultative sense). The prepositional phrase 'به شمشیر سخت' uses 'به' as an instrument/agent marker here ('by/with the severe sword'), so the line says his heart was made weary/wounded by the harsh sword; 'سخت' postmodifies 'شمشیر' as 'severe/harsh'.
segments.s507-g103: 'چو آمد' = 'when he came' (past). 'به لشکرگه خویش' is 'to his army-camp' where 'خویش' is formed from 'خوی' + enclitic 'ش' = 'his own'; the possessor is attached as an enclitic. 'باز' is the adverb 'again' placed after the locative phrase (poetic word order) to mean 'came again'.
segments.s507-g104: 'برفتند' is 3rd-person plural past 'they went away/they departed'. The noun phrase 'گردان گردن‌فراز' follows and names who departed: 'گردان' = 'troop/unit' and 'گردن‌فراز' is a compound adjective 'neck‑raising' = 'proud' (an epithet). The verb-subject order (V + noun phrase) reflects poetic inversion.
segments.s507-g57: چو is a subordinating particle here meaning 'when/since'. باید is a modal verb 'must/ought' placed before the object phrase; پرستندگان را uses را as the definite/object marker attached to 'the devotees'. خورش is a predicative noun ('a stew / cooked dish') that the clause says ought to be the devotees' food — Persian poetry allows this fronting/order.
segments.s507-g58: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. گوشت means 'meat/flesh'. ددان here would normally mean 'beasts' in context, but the provided lemma senses do not match that meaning (so marked -1). باشدش is the present-tense/hortative-archaic form 'it is/let it be to it' with the enclitic -ش referring back to the thing being described; پرورش is used as 'nourishment/raising' (the source or sustenance).
segments.s507-g59: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. بر + تیغ گنبد is a prepositional phrase 'upon/on the blade (ridge) of the dome' and رسید is the past verb 'reached/arrived' placed at the end as is normal in Persian clauses.
segments.s507-g60: سکندر is the subject (Alexander). ز بالا is a simple prepositional phrase 'from above' using poetic ز for 'from'. خروشی is the object 'a roar/clamour' and شنید is the past-tense verb 'heard' in third person singular.
segments.s507-g61: که is the relative/complementizer 'which/that' linking back to the sound mentioned earlier. آمد is the past verb 'came'. ز is the preposition 'from', and برگ درخت بلند is the noun phrase 'the leaf of a tall tree' (درخت 'tree' followed by the adjective بلند 'tall').
segments.s507-g62: خروشی is the noun 'a roar/commotion'. پر + از is the idiom 'full of' (پر here means 'full' and combines with از 'of'). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. ناسودمند is an adjectival compound meaning 'unprofitable / fruitless'; it qualifies the nature of the roar.
segments.s507-g63: بترسید is a past-tense verb 'he feared / became afraid' (third person). و links the two verbs. پرسید is 'he asked'. زان is a demonstrative/prepositional combining 'from that / about that' referring to the sound, and ترجمان is the noun 'interpreter' (the addressee of the question).
segments.s507-g64: که‌ای is an older/poetic particle introducing direct speech or an address (here functioning like 'saying' or 'O'). مرد بیدار is the vocative 'O man, awake/alert' (noun + adjective). نیکی گمان is a terse idiom literally 'good thought' or 'good belief' and here functions as an exhortation meaning roughly 'regard it as a good omen / think well of it.'
segments.s507-g33: An affirmative opening + past verb phrase: 'چنین' is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed at the start for emphasis; 'داد' is the past tense verb 'gave'; 'پاسخ' is the direct object 'answer/reply'; 'بدو' is the indirect object 'to him'; 'ترجمان' (interpreter) is the agent/subject appearing after the verb, a common postverbal positioning in older Persian poetic word order.
segments.s507-g34: 'که' introduces a subordinate clause ('that/that when...'); 'از روز' literally 'from the day' (classical phrasing), and 'چون بگذرد' here functions as a temporal clause 'when/as soon as it passes' (classical use of چون = 'when' — not listed in the supplied senses); the clause ends with the nominal phrase 'نه زمان' (lit. 'not time'), an elliptical verbless construction meaning 'there will be little/no time' or 'no long delay' (the copula/auxiliary is omitted as is common in poetry).
segments.s507-g35: 'سخن‌گو' is a compound noun 'speaker' (literally 'speech-mouth'); 'گردد' is the classical present/future-like verb 'becomes/will become' (used here as prophetic future); 'یکی' is an indefinite 'one/someone/a certain one' placed after the verb; 'زین' is a contracted classical form meaning 'از این' ('from this') and links immediately to 'درخت' ('tree') — overall: 'a speaker will become (arise) from this tree.'
segments.s507-g36: 'که' here is a relative pronoun 'whose/that' introducing the clause that characterizes the speaker; 'آواز او' = 'his/her voice' (possessive pronoun 'او' follows the noun); 'بشنود' is the classical third-person verb 'hears/will hear' (subjunctive/optative shape but here used as simple present/future in narration); 'نیک‌بخت' ('fortunate one') is the subject/agent of 'بشنود' placed after the verb (postverbal subject), so the clause reads literally 'the fortunate one will hear his voice.'
segments.s507-g37: 'شب تیره‌گون' is a noun + adjective 'dark-hued night' (adjective follows noun); 'ماده' here means 'the female' (likely 'the female [creature/subject]' rather than 'matter'); 'گویا شود' is a causative/resultative construction meaning 'becomes eloquent/speaking' or 'becomes articulate' — 'شود' is the auxiliary 'becomes' used to form the intransitive predicate, so the clause says 'on a dark night the female becomes speaking.'
segments.s507-g38: 'بر و برگ' is an idiomatic pair meaning 'twigs/branches and leaves' (a fixed collocation); 'چون' here is the comparative/simile 'like/as' linking to 'مشک' ('musk'); 'بویا شود' is a verbal phrase meaning 'becomes fragrant' — 'بویا' functions as an adjective 'scented/fragrant' and 'شود' is 'becomes', so the whole segment means 'and leaves/foliage become as fragrant as musk.'
segments.s507-g39: 'بپرسید' is an imperative/optative form ('ask!' or in older narrative sometimes rendered as 'he asked'); here it functions as an imperative direction 'ask'; 'چون بگذریم' is a temporal subordinate clause 'when we pass' (چون used classically as 'when' — not matched in the supplied senses); 'از درخت' 'by/from the tree' marks the object/place passed — together 'when we pass by the tree.'
segments.s507-g40: Interrogative main clause: 'شگفتی' 'a wonder/marvel' is the topic; 'چه' fronted interrogative 'what'; 'پیش آید' is a verbal phrase 'will come about / will happen' (پیش as adverb + آید 'comes/happens'); the phrase ends with the vocative 'ای نیک‌بخت' ('O fortunate one'), addressing the addressee — so the clause asks 'what marvel will occur, O fortunate one?'

sentences.s507-s9: He asked them, "What wonder is here, what thing is there that ought to be taken to that extent?"
sentences.s507-s10: The guide thus gave him this answer: "O victorious, pure-minded king..."
sentences.s507-s11: There is a marvel here that in the world no one has seen, neither in public nor in secret.
sentences.s507-s12: Here is a tree that has become two-trunked, a paired tree, for such a marvel cannot be concealed.
sentences.s507-s1: From the desert road he came to a city and became glad when he heard the voice of the people.
sentences.s507-s2: The whole land and countryside were full of flourishing gardens; among the people there was joy from mirth.
sentences.s507-s3: The city's great men received him — anyone who was one of the people for (their) sake.
sentences.s507-s4: His comrades shouted 'Bravo!' and they scattered all their gold and jewels.
sentences.s507-s57: Each gemstone was worth a dirham and as large as my thumb; one rhinoceros (figure) was of gold and jewels.
sentences.s507-s58: He took that city and put the army to flight; from his eyes he scattered the blood of his heart.
sentences.s507-s25: The fortunate one rode on with the Romans; when he came near, it seemed like a tree.
sentences.s507-s26: Its ground, from the heat, kept lifting up; from the hides of the beasts no soil could be seen.
sentences.s507-s27: He asked the speaker, 'What is this skin? Who is the ferocious one in that manner?'
sentences.s507-s28: Thus the fortunate one answered him: 'The tree has many worshippers.'
sentences.s507-s13: One was female and the other male; its bough was a speaker, with color and scent.
sentences.s507-s14: At night the female becomes speaking and scented; when it becomes light, the male becomes speaking.
sentences.s507-s15: Alexander set out with the horsemen of Rome, those renowned men of that border and land.
sentences.s507-s16: He asked of them: 'Now, when will the tree utter speech in a harsh voice?'
sentences.s507-s45: Ask the king's interpreter, O clear‑hearted and pious man.
sentences.s507-s46: Ask an investigator whether I should go to Rum when that turning of the day comes.
sentences.s507-s47: Unless my mother sees me alive, let someone lift my veil to her face.
sentences.s507-s48: Thus he spoke to the king, as if a tree: 'Shorten the day and bundle up your things.'
sentences.s507-s53: They set out gifts into the city's inner quarter; the nobles hastened toward the king.
sentences.s507-s54: There was one coat of mail, shining like indigo; in height and breadth it equaled the hide of an elephant.
sentences.s507-s55: It had two elephant tusks and five sections, which made it a hardship to lift.
sentences.s507-s56: There was armor and a rich brocade; made of gold, it was filled with a hundred khāye.
sentences.s507-s33: Thus the speaking leaf kept saying that the heart was turning into a stream of blood.
sentences.s507-s34: Thus he answered, 'O fortunate one' — this leaf of the bough keeps saying.
sentences.s507-s35: that how many an Alexander the world breeds who takes from his good deeds for his own end.
sentences.s507-s36: When his kingship had become a year and two weeks, he was obliged to leave the throne of greatness.
sentences.s507-s37: Blood streamed from Alexander's eyes; his heart became full of pain because of the guide.
sentences.s507-s38: After that he opened his lips to no one; his lips remained full of sorrow until midnight.
sentences.s507-s39: Another leaf of the tree became a speaker; again it asked that fortunate one.
sentences.s507-s40: What does this other branch say? — the speaker revealed the hidden secret.
sentences.s507-s21: He answered thus: “If you pass by him, by your leaving the judgment will be shortened.”
sentences.s507-s22: When you passed by him no place remained; the guide called him at the edge of the world.
sentences.s507-s23: Wilderness and darkness came before; no one came away from his life satisfied.
sentences.s507-s24: No one of us saw it, nor ever heard that trap and beasts and bird leaped onto the road.
sentences.s507-s5: Each one kept saying, "O king, leave to us what you have done to Anoushe."
sentences.s507-s6: No army ever came to this city, nor has anyone ever heard the name of a king.
sentences.s507-s7: Now the wish of our heart has come before you; may you be bright‑souled and have a sound body.
sentences.s507-s8: Alexander made the people’s hearts glad, and by the desert road he freed his body.
sentences.s507-s41: She answered thus: that this main bough constantly speaks throughout the wide world.
sentences.s507-s42: You cannot be contained by excessive greed; why would you put the soul upon torment?
sentences.s507-s43: Your greed is to roam the world — to harm people and to kill kings.
sentences.s507-s44: Do not linger here long; do not make your days narrow and tight upon yourself.
sentences.s507-s49: Neither your mother nor your kinsmen will see you in Rome, nor the veiled-faced folk of that land.
sentences.s507-s50: In the city among the people your death will come — it will not be late; the star, the crown and the throne will become sated with you.
sentences.s507-s51: When he heard it, he turned back from those two trees; his heart had become weary by the severe sword.
sentences.s507-s52: When he came again to his own camp, the proud troop departed.
sentences.s507-s29: Since the devotees' food ought to be a stew, its nourishment comes from the flesh of beasts.
sentences.s507-s30: When the sun reached the dome's ridge like a blade, Alexander heard a roar from above.
sentences.s507-s31: Which came from the leaf of a tall tree — a roar full of claim and unprofitableness.
sentences.s507-s32: He was afraid and asked that interpreter, saying, 'O alert man, take it as a good omen.'
sentences.s507-s17: Thus the interpreter gave him this answer: that when the day passes, there will be no (long) time / no delay.
sentences.s507-s18: A speaker will arise from this tree, whose voice the fortunate one will hear.
sentences.s507-s19: On a dark-hued night the female becomes vocal, and leaves and foliage become fragrant like musk.
sentences.s507-s20: Ask (tell them), O fortunate one: what wonder will occur when we pass by the tree?
